courses.subject: SOCI
courses.title: Introduction to Sociology
courses.description: This course introduces basic concepts and principles in the field of sociology, helping students to make sense of their social life. Ideas such as social norms, social roles, social interactions, social institutions and social processes will be examined. This course also gives a brief introduction to major classical and contemporary sociological theories which explain how individuals and society are related to each other, especially how personal experiences are rooted in a particular social setting. Though these discussions, students should be able to think more critically toward a wide range of social issues and taken-for-granted social practices.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Not for students who have taken SOCI1102 or UGEC1823.

courses.subject: SOCI
courses.title: Approaching Sociology
courses.description: Designed for major students new to sociology, this course serves as an extended introduction to sociology’s distinctive approach to the study of social life. It also aims at setting the foundation for the development of a variety of skills required for the study of discipline. It is also part of the Sociology Department’s academic mentorship programme that helps students better adjust to studying sociology, for example to learn to write better academic essay, and plan for themselves in their undergraduate years.
courses.learning_outcomes:
(1) Familiarize with the sociological approach to social phenomena and the distinctive vocabulary and perspectives by which sociologists study them.

(2) Learn how to solve concrete self-directed research problems in a collaborative team setting and acquire first-hand experience in how social research is conducted.

(3) Become aware of the importance of a variety of study skills highlighted in the programme learning outcomes and the channels by which those skills could be developed in undergraduate studies.

(4) Better adjust to studying in the sociology programme in particular and plan for themselves in their undergraduate years.

(5) Be prepared to critically reflect on the role of AI tools and resources in their academic journey throughout university.
courses.course_syllabus:
1. The curriculum will be individualized by the instructors responsible for each section. Section instructors will discuss and review the curriculum annually.
2. In addition to effort to coordinate the individualized curriculum, the course includes two standardized components for two of the learning outcomes:
a. All instructors of this course will serve as Level I Academic Advisors for their students during the current academic year. Instructor will structure their courses to include designated contact hours specifically allocated for academic advising, enabling students to better plan their studies and university life. A standardized academic advising guideline, based on Annex 9 of the Handbook on the Implementation of the Academic Advisory System, will be distributed to course instructors prior to the start of the term. (Learning Outcome 4)

b. To prepare students for critical reflection on the use of AI tools throughout their university studies, three standardized micro-modules will be provided to course instructors by the department for integration into their course: the first covering general AI literacy for social science students, the second outlining the CUHK AI Policy, and the third focusing on the critical and ethical use of AI as learning companion. Students are expected to complete and engage these self-guided modules as part of their course training. (Learning Outcome 5)

courses.subject: SOCI
courses.title: Sociology and Modern Society
courses.description:
(UGEC1823 is double-coded with SOCI1102.)

This course explores into the significance of sociological perspectives in understanding modern society. Attention will be given to the application of sociological concepts in the observation and analysis of social phenomena in modern society. The course is designed particularly to develop intellectual skills, as part of a student's general education, for a better understanding of the nature of modern society.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Not for students who have taken SOCI1001 or UGEC1823.
courses.learning_outcomes:
- Students will gain exposure to a diverse set of sociological research, and understand how the relevant concepts and theories can be applied to modern societies.

- Students will develop an understanding of the constitutive relations between the economic, political, social, and cultural domains in society, thereby gaining a new perspective on their own lives and the world around them.

- Students will learn to develop their own analytical framework through active participation in discussion and completion of essay type test questions. These will enhance their general knowledge and skills in critical thinking.
courses.course_syllabus:
CORE CONTENT

1. Sociology as a social science discipline

- What is a social science explanation?

- Variety of sociological explanations

2. Rise of modernity

- With special reference to the insights of Durkheim, Weber, and Marx.

3. The Internet society and globalization

- Nature of postindustrial society and information society

- Virtual community v. traditional community

- The small world phenomenon: Insights from social network research

4. Consumer society

- Nature of advanced capitalism

- Economic sociology of market demand

5. Marriage and family

- The changing conceptions of marriage and consequences of the changes

- Theoretical debates among such theories as sociobiology, social conflict, rational choice

6. Socioeconomic inequality and status competition in modern society

- The root sources of inequality: classic and contemporary theories

- Rising inequality in the USA and East Asia

- Status competition

7. Selected topics

- For examples: Theories and evidence on modern schooling, gender and work, deviance and social control, social movement, democratization, health and health care, third world development.

courses.subject: SOCI
courses.title: Hong Kong Society: Identities and Boundaries
courses.description:
From the “borrowed time and borrowed place”, Hong Kong has evolved into a global city with its own identity. Since the 1950s, the city experienced an economic miracle, withstood numerous financial crises, and is in the process of economic restructuring. Politically, it has turned from “the last Colony” of Great Britain into a Special Administrative Region of the PRC. Culturally, the flourishing Canto pop-culture coincides with the emergence of a local identity. Using a sociological perspective, this course critically reflects on the impact of these profound changes on the construction of the so-called ‘Hong Kong identity’. Who is a HongKongese? This seemingly simple question underlies critical issues and boundaries about nationality, ethnicity, language, culture, class, and citizenship. The construction of a Hong Kong identity necessitates the drawing of boundaries that include and exclude, defines who is entitled to resources and power and who is not, and projects differential expectations of rights and responsibilities on insiders and outsiders. The question thus has profound impacts on how resources and power are distributed in society, and affects the life chances of individuals, families and society.
(Advisory: Not for Sociology Majors.)
courses.enrollment_requirement: Not for students who have taken UGEC2865/GEJC1020.
courses.learning_outcomes:
本課程期望達到以下五個學習成果:

1. 希望透過本課程, 以社會學的視野去幫助同學對香港戰後經歷過的經濟發展, 政治變化, 及文化上的衝擊有更深入及批判性的了解;

2. 使同學能夠全面思考身份的建構如何受社會, 經濟和政治環境影響, 並明白身份建構背所涉的權利與義務, 和政治與經濟資源分配.

3. 以一個社會變遷 (social change) 的角度去討論身份. 使同學明白香港人這一身份的建構與香港過去半世紀的社會改變, 包括本土化, 去工業化, 經濟轉型, 全球化, 新中產階級的興起, 主權移交等的關係.

4. 以一個較宏觀全面的角度去了解 ‘香港人’ 這個身份是如何受社會結構 (social structure) 與個人的互動的影響; 並啟發學生思考‘個人身份’ (individual identity)與群體 (groups), 社區(communities), 社會系統, (systems), 和制度 (institutions) 的關係.

5. 透過多樣化的學習方式, 包括嘉賓講者,多媒體影音教材播放及討論, 和小組專題習作 使同學對香港社會的歷史, 結構, 文化和在這個社會裡生活的不同社群有一個較深入的了解, 亦能學習到以較全面宏觀的角度去思考問題.
courses.course_syllabus:
１. 導論

２. 身份與界限

３. 半世紀的變革：由歷史開始, 誰的歷史?

４. 本土化與香港人身份的建構

５. “新移民”身份的污名化

６. 香港社會研究: 理論與實踐

７. 貧窮與身份

８. 中產階級身份危機與新一代的文化資本戰

９. 認識香港的上層階級, 解讀統治霸權

１０. 與弱勢社群對話–香港的小數族裔(巴基斯坦青年和志願組織)

１１. 五十萬的外籍傭工: 種族,性別與階級的角力

１２. 性小眾的邊緣化

１３. 挑戰社會不平等: 由新社會運動建立的另類集體身份

１４. 一個小結

courses.subject: SOCI
courses.title: Social Research Methods
courses.description: This course introduces the fundamental principles and procedures of empirical research in sociology. We will address the theoretical and practical challenges involved in problem formulation, research design, data collection, and data analysis. The course will cover major types of both quantitative and qualitative social research methods, such as survey research, qualitative interviews, and ethnography. We will also discuss sampling and measurement, and analysis issues associated with their use. Students are required to conduct and report on a basic research project using quantitative and qualitative methods.
courses.learning_outcomes:
After completing this course, students should be able to:

1. Explain the process of scientific inquiry and the steps in social research.
2. Compare and contrast various methods of conducting social research.
3. Evaluate the reliability and validity of research findings.
4. Formulate a research project using appropriate research design.

1. 2. Nature and process of social research

2. Conceptual Issues in Social Research

2. 3. Quality criteria in social research

3. Building Blocks of Social Research

4. Quantitative Research

4. 2. Survey research

5. 2. In-depth interviewing

6. 2. Content analysis

7. Sampling in social research

7. 3. Sampling in student projects

Chung Chi College Lib CK TSE

courses.description: This course introduces the basic principles of statistical reasoning and its applications in social research. The objectives are to understand the basic assumptions of statistical methods and to interpret statistical findings in social surveys and experiments. Emphasis will be given to those descriptive and inferential methods that are frequently used for analyzing causal relationships among social variables and for testing social theories and hypotheses. The course will train students in analyzing social life and its changes.

courses.learning_outcomes:

2. To appreciate how statistical methods can be used to study social phenomena.

3. To be able to design and conduct a real empirical research with quantitative method.

4. To develop the quantitatively analytical and critical-thinking skills to current social issues.

5. To acquire hands-on knowledge on using statistical software to manage real socioeconomic data.

courses.subject: SOCI
courses.title: Sociology and Hong Kong
courses.description: The objective of this course is to review and reflect on the efforts made by sociologists to understand the development of the Hong Kong society over the past half century. What is so special about Hong Kong? Why did it fascinate sociologists for so long in the post-war era? What had been the questions that caught their imagination? Has Hong Kong been falling out of the radar of sociologists? What do sociologists know about Hong Kong, and what more have to be done?
courses.enrollment_requirement: Not for students who have taken SOCI1201, UGEC2865 or GEJC1020.
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. To understand and reflect on the contribution of sociology in the understanding of Hong Kong.
2. To master the dominant themes of sociological research on Hong Kong society.
3. To familiarize with and learn how to draw on the common primary and secondary sources of sociological research on Hong Kong society.

4. Integrating personal interests with existing research, to develop a personal agenda on the understanding of Hong Kong society.

5. To locate sociological research on Hong Kong from a developmental and comparative perspective.
courses.course_syllabus:
1. Sociology in HK
2. A Sense of History: Beyond colonialism
3. Politics: SK Lau and Beyond
4. Economy: The Laissez-faire Miracle?
5. Class & Mobility: Land of Opportunity?
6. Globalization and Inequality
7. The HK Family: From Tradition to Modernity
8. Culture and Identity: From Left-Right to HK-China
9. Social Movement: The Rise, Fall, and Revival of Social Movements
10. Cultural Industry: The Boom nd Bust of Hong Kong Movies
11. Conclusion: What shall we do from here?

courses.subject: SOCI
courses.title: Economic Reform and Social Impacts in China
courses.description:
(UGEC2883 is double-coded with SOCI2106.)

Focusing on economic reforms, students will be taught the impetus of the institutional changes in China and how they affect the cultural, socioeconomic and organizational lives of Chinese people. Key social science concepts and theories like institution, ideology, socialism, marketization, social control, civil society, industrialization, urbanization, stratification, family, socialization, and specialized terms in China studies like guanxi (關係), danwei (單位), hukou (戶口), harmonious society, China dream, floating population, rural-urban divide, one/two child(ren) policy, will be introduced and discussed from an integrated perspective. After taking this course, students should be able to grasp the transformation and development in key areas of China, including economic reform, political system and change, rural and urban development, social inequality, family and marriage, cultural and ideological issues, as well as other institutional developments.
courses.enrollment_requirement:
SOCI2106 is double-coded with UGEC2883. 
Not for students who have taken GESC2118, UGEA2190 or UGEC2883.
courses.learning_outcomes:
• Students are expected to grasp the basic knowledge about China’s major social institutions such as socialist market economy, party-state apparatus, stratification system, and lineage values. They should also be able to understand the evolution of these institutions over the past several decades.

• Students should be able to understand how social scientists analyze and examine the formation, evolution, and influences of the major social institutions in contemporary China. They are expected to grasp the key concepts and theories developed from social scientists from sociology, economics, political science, etc.

• Besides basic facts and social science perspectives, students are encouraged to critically evaluate some controversial topics from different angles through writing assignments. The topics will include key dilemmas in contemporary China regarding economic growth and social equality, state capacity and democracy, traditional culture and modernization, etc.
courses.course_syllabus:
* *Week 1: Introduction: Basic Information of the Chinese Society**

\* Provide basic information about the socio-economic characteristics of the contemporary China.

* *Week 2 & 3: China before Reform Era**

\* Provide information about the political systems, economic institutions, and social control mechanisms of China during Mao’s era.

* *Week 4** **& 5: Economic Reform**

\* Provide information about the processes of economic reform since 1978, as well as its impact on the economic system afterwards.

* *Week 6 & 7 & 8:** **Impacts on the Political System and the Preservation of the Party-state**

\* Provide information about the impact of the economic reform on the political system in China afterwards. Also, we will discuss the puzzle regarding the preservation of the Party-state despite the massive political reform.

* *Week 9****: Impacts on Rural-urban Development and Migration**

\* Provide information about the differential impact of the economic reform on the urban and rural development, as well as the resultant great migration since then.

* *Week 10: Impacts on Social Stratification and Inequality**

\* Provide information about the impact of the economic reform on the transition of the social stratification system and the enlarging social inequality.

* *Week 11 & 12: Impacts on Social Conflicts, Civil Society, and Social Control**

\* Provide information about the impact of the economic reform on the growing social conflicts and the newly emerged social control mechanisms.

* *Week 13: Impacts on Population and Demographic Transition**

\* Provide information about the impact of the economic reform on the population and demographic patterns.

* *[\*whether covering this depending on our schedule, because we may save some time for students to finish the final paper before the due date\*]**

courses.subject: SOCI
courses.title: Urban Sociology
courses.description: Sociology has always had an intimate relationship with cities. Urbanization — the increase of the share of the population living in cities — was one of the developments that early sociologists sought to study and explain. Since then, the global urban population has only continued to increase. Today, over half of humanity lives in cities and towns, making urban society as essential to understand now as it was in the nineteenth and twentieth centuries. Urban sociology has many sides, but in this course, students will learn about four main topics: 1) Early theories and classic statements on urban life, 2) the distinctive social character of the “urban experience,” 3) understanding urban space through the political economy approach, and 4) examining urban spatial processes within and beyond Hong Kong, including affordable housing, gentrification, the creative class, and minorities in the city.
courses.learning_outcomes:
After completing this course, students should be able to:

1. Describe the distinctive social character of the urban experience
2. Examine urban development patterns through both physical infrastructure and social dimensions with attention to local and global contexts
3. Analyze urban phenomena through multiple theoretical frameworks and across different international contexts
courses.course_syllabus:

3. The Urban Experience

4. Contextualizing Urban Spatial Process
* Do we have Gentrification in Hong Kong?
* Do we have Ghettos in Hong Kong?
5. Political Economy Approach
* Critical Urban Theory

6. Global Cities and the Creative Class

courses.subject: SOCI
courses.title: Youth and Society: Issues in the Contemporary World
courses.description: This course explores some of the major issues concerning the role of the youth and the problems confronting them in modern society. The nature of the conventional wisdom of the “youth problem” is critically examined, with the help of concepts and theories in sociology and related fields. By the end of the course, students are expected to gain more understanding of the nature of the phenomenon of problematization of youth, and to look beyond common sense or public perception on current social issues related to the youth in modern society. More importantly, youth problems can jeopardize the sustainable development of youth. A broader and deeper understanding of various facets of problematization can inform how youth should be empowered to attain sustainable development in modern society.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Not for students who have taken UGEC2703.
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. To capture the extent of youth issues in modern society.
2. To demonstrate the ability to analyze complex youth problems through evaluating major arguments and evidence in the social studies of youth.
3. To critically assess how public perception of youth issues are shaped by social relations and institutions, patterns of social diversity and inequality, and processes that underpin social change and stability.
4. To reflect on students’ own life experiences as members of a younger population and their challenges of sustainable development in modern society.
5. To explore contemporary youth policy taking into consideration the dynamic local, national and global context.
courses.course_syllabus:
The core content of the course covers five areas in the study of youth issues.

1. The developmental discourse and the concept of social construction are examined to illustrate the emergence and the nature of the youth concept.
2. Biological, psychological and sociological views on the conceptualization of youth problem are introduced and compared, so as to illustrate the role and focus of social science, sociology in particular, in the study of youth problem.
3. Specific youth topics such as (1) education experience and inequality, (2) school-to-work transition, (3) generations and social mobility, (4) youth subculture, (5) gender and inequality, (6) love, intimacy and wellbeing, (7) sexual minorities, (8) digital lives and challenges, and (9) normalization of risk behaviors, are explored to scrutinize the forms and the extent of issues encountered by youth in modern society.
4. Sociological concepts and perspectives are discussed to illuminate the social factors affecting the above-mentioned youth issues. Postmodern perspectives and concepts will also be introduced to offer a discussion on the recent emergence of new forms of social transformation and crisis faced by youth.

The relevancy of topics and sustainable development goals (SDGs) are highlighted below:

Topic 1 Conceptualizing Youth

Topic 2 Conceptualization of Youth Problem

Topic 3 Education Experience and Inequality (SDG 4 Quality Education)

Topic 4 School-to-Work Transition (SDG8 Decent Work and Economic Growth)

Topic 5 Generation and Social Mobility (SDG1 No Poverty; SDG10 Reduced Inequalities)

Topic 6 Youth Subculture (SDG3 Good Health and Well-Being; SDG16 Peace, Justice and Strong Institutions)

Topic 7 Gender and Inequality (SDG 5 Gender Equality)

Topic 8 Love, Intimacy and Wellbeing (SDG3 Good Health and Well-Being)

Topic 9 Sexual Minorities (SDG 5 Gender Equality)

Topic 10 Digital Lives and Challenges (SDG3 Good Health and Well-Being)

Topic 11 Youth in a Risk Society (SDG3 Good Health and Well-Being; SDG16 Peace, Justice and Strong Institutions)

Topic 12 Conclusion: Citizenship and Belonging (SDG17 Partnerships for the Goals; SDG16 Peace, Justice and Strong Institutions)

courses.subject: SOCI
courses.title: Introduction to Chinese Law and Society
courses.description: This course provides an overview of different topics in the fields of law, crime and society, including responsive regulation, cyberfraud crime, environmental crime, white-collar crime, financial crime, climate justice, law and technology, global governance, and transnational and international law. It will cover how empirical data are used to examine the theoretical concepts, such as regulatory governance, restorative justice, motivational posturing, reintegrative shaming, and procedural justice. In addition to understanding the ideas in the readings, students are expected to explore the latest practices in a particular area.
courses.learning_outcomes:
By the end of this course, students will be able to:

1. **Understand the key concepts of law, crime and society:** Grasp the key sociological, sociolegal, and criminological concepts to understand actors, institutions, and procedures in the contemporary Chinese judicial system. (LO1)
2. **Make sociological, sociolegal, or criminological explanations of regulatory and compliance behaviors:** Use sociological, sociolegal, and criminological concepts to examine the private sector's compliance activities and the public sector’s regulatory enforcement practices. (LO2) And,
3. **Contrive the latest practices based on empirical studies**: develop an empirical study of the latest practices in a particular area and provide sociological, sociolegal, or criminological explanations of such practices. (LO3)

courses.subject: SOCI
courses.title: Social Stratification
courses.description: This course discusses principles of allocation and reallocation of valuable social resources among different sectors of the population. Topics include fundamental concepts of social stratification, historical development of social inequality, classical and contemporary theories of class, social mobility, contradiction between equality and equity, implications of labour market segmentation and globalization on social equality.
courses.learning_outcomes:
Untitled Document

This course will review some of the classical and contemporary approaches to understanding the differential distribution of valued goods and the social processes. Students are encouraged to critically think of and discuss about six categories of questions as below:

(a) What are the major forms of stratification in human history? Is inequality an inevitable feature of human life?

(b) How many social classes are there? What are the principal? "fault lines" or social cleavages that define the class structure? Are these cleavages strengthening or weakening with the transition to advanced industrialism?

(c) How frequently do individuals cross occupational or class boundaries? Are educational degrees, social contacts, and "individual luck" important forces in matching individuals to jobs and class positions? What other social and institutional forces affect occupational attainment and allocation?

(d) How are the lifestyles, attitudes, and personalities of individuals shaped by their class locations? Are there identifiable "class cultures" in past and present societies?

(e) What types of social processes and state policies serve to maintain or alter racial, ethnic, and sex discrimination in labor markets? Have these forms of discrimination weakened or strengthened with the transition to advanced industrialism?

(f) Will stratification systems take on new and distinctive forms in the future? Is a "new class" of professionals and intellectuals emerging? Are the stratification systems of modern societies gradually shedding their distinctive features and converging towards some generic post-industrial or postmodern regime?

1. Introduction

courses.subject: SOCI
courses.title: Classical Sociological Theory
courses.description: This course explores the foundational works of classical sociological theory through the writings of Émile Durkheim (French, 1858-1917), Karl Marx (German, 1818-1883), and Max Weber (German, 1864-1920). Students will examine how these theorists addressed fundamental social issues and developed frameworks for analyzing social phenomena. The course emphasizes critical reading, analytical thinking, and understanding the historical context of sociological concepts. This course will explore the following questions: 1. What key themes and ideas emerge from classical sociological theory? 2. How does their social and intellectual context shape these ideas? 3. How can these theories inform our sociological way of questioning?
courses.learning_outcomes:
After completing this course, students should be able to:

1. Explain the key ideas of major classical sociological theories and perspectives
2. Apply foundational concepts and frameworks of sociological theory
3. Examine historical and contemporary social issues from sociological perspectives
4. Evaluate critically theoretical arguments and their connections to empirical evidence

courses.subject: SOCI
courses.title: Social Networks and Social Capital
courses.description: Social networks affect many aspects of our lives, and their effect persists over the life course. The science of social networks focuses on measuring, modeling, and understanding the different ways that people are connected to one another. In this course, we will use a broad toolkit of theories and methods drawn from the social, natural, and mathematical sciences to learn what a social network is, to understand how to work with social network data, and to illustrate some of the ways that social networks can be useful in theory and in practice. This course is designed to provide students with the opportunity to (i) master the major concepts within the field of social networks, with a particular attention to social capital theory; (ii) critically examine the causes and consequences of social networks across a variety of behavioral contexts and academic domains; and (iii) achieve minimum competency in social network analysis.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Not for students who have taken UGEC2013.
courses.learning_outcomes:
By the end of the course, you should be able to:

(1) Understand basic network concepts and theories, (2) Use social-scientific terminology to describe patterns of structure and change observed in networks, (3) Understand and be capable of using measures based on graph theory and matrix algebra to analyze and describe social networks, (4) Understand the way that social network concepts, theory, and measures can be applied to shed light on a wide variety of phenomena across different fields of study in the social sciences and beyond.

courses.subject: SOCI
courses.title: Gender and Society
courses.description:
This course takes up gender as the primary object of study and category of analysis. It aims to:
1) understand the conceptualization of sex and gender by various scholars and the implications of their theorization; 2) understand the process of becoming a woman/ man via the social construction of masculinity, femininity, sexuality and the body;3) understand the implications of being a woman/man on our lived experience, and the mechanisms that reproduce and maintain the observed gaps between the two sexes in daily lives or various institutions, e.g. family, education, and labour markets etc.
courses.learning_outcomes:
Students in this course should study and demonstrate understanding of the following: the discipline of sociology and its role in contributing to our understanding of social order and its changes; the role of theory in sociology; the role of evidence and

Students in this course should study and demonstrate understanding of the following: the discipline of sociology and its role in contributing to our understanding of social order and its changes; the role of theory in sociology; the role of evidence and qualitative methods in sociology; basics concepts in sociology and their fundamental theoretical interrelations; critical thinking; further develop transferable skills beyond disciplinary requirements; development of values, such that the student will see the utility of the sociological perspective as a useful and meaningful perspective on social reality and individual biography, for reflection of value positions and life choices.

courses.subject: SOCI
courses.title: Contemporary Sociological Theory
courses.description:
Most every sociology course on a specific domain of social life includes a discussion of the major theories proposed by sociologists in that specialty. This course is different. Here, students will learn not about theories of some specific sociological specialty, but instead contemporary sociological theories — period, full stop. What is offered by this course is an introduction to perspectives on society that link together the different facets of social life. These perspectives claim that the same insights can illuminate not just, say, education but also work and the family. They represent lens that can be used to view and understand these various social spheres, or they identify common trends that affect them. Students will gain from this course a deeper understanding of the most general questions asked by sociologists today, and which link together the more narrowly focused view of each individual study or subfield.
We strongly encourage students to take SOCI3003 before taking this course.
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. To build skills in reading and discussing conceptually sophisticated arguments about society.
2. To acquire familiarity with the major theorists, theorists, and enduring questions of sociological theory in the past several decades.
3. To enhance the capacity to confront social life from a critical viewpoint through the application of theoretical perspectives to concrete examples.

courses.subject: SOCI
courses.title: Social Demography
courses.description: This course investigates social demographic phenomena in both structural and dynamic aspects. Causes and consequences of demographic trends at local, national and global levels will be examined. Subjects like population theories; determinants of population dynamics such as fertility, mortality and migration; and the integration of social and demographic theories will also be discussed.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Not for students who have taken UGEC2033.
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. To stimulate students’ interest in the science of demography.

2. Not simply to teach demography, but how to learn demography through an introduction to the perspectives and tools of demography.

courses.subject: SOCI
courses.title: Quantitative Data Analysis
courses.description: The focus of this course is on hands-on data analysis and the practical application of basic statistical methods to real-world social problems. Topics include causality, measurement, probability and statistical theory, regression models, analyses of network, textual, and spatial data. Particular attention will be paid to learning and implementing the R statistical program. Having taken Social Statistics (SOCI 2004 or equivalent elsewhere) and prior computing experience might be helpful but not required.
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. To master the logic of statistical analysis, with practical knowledge with a statistical program.
2. To rigorously apply statistical inference and estimation methods to real-world problems.
3. To be familiar with automated methods and visualization tools needed to identify patterns in network, textual, and spatial data or “big data.”
4. To be a keen reader and critical consumer of quantitative social science research.

courses.subject: SOCI
courses.title: Qualitative Research
courses.description: This course aims to provide students with a foundation in the fundamental principles and procedures of empirical research in sociology. It will introduce various major types of qualitative research methods. Students will learn about various strategies for collecting (e.g. in-depth interview/focus-group discussion, participant observation/ethnography, and unobtrusive methods) and analyzing qualitative data (e.g. analyses of textual data as well as grounded theory). They will also familiarize themselves with the ethical issues about conducting research in contemporary times.
courses.learning_outcomes:
(1) understand the logic of scientific inquiry and the steps in social research

(2) describe and explain the essential characteristics of major qualitative research methods

(3) compare and evaluate the relative strengths and weaknesses of major qualitative research methods

(4) design and implement qualitative research project to address research questions

(5) evaluate quality of a qualitative research project

(6) reflect on the ethical issues related to qualitative research

courses.subject: SOCI
courses.title: Digital Sociology
courses.description: Data and computational revolutions, coupled with the emergence of artificial intelligence (AI), have significantly impacted social life and request a new approach to the epistemic, methodological and normative issues of computational social science as an interdisciplinary field. This course introduces digital forms of research that advances theories of human behaviour by leveraging large-scale data from social media, dynamic networks, and real-time digitised and administrative records. This course also practices sociological imaginations to understand new social problems that we face in the age of big data and AI.
courses.learning_outcomes:
By the end of this course, students should be able to:

* Describe the opportunities and challenges that the digital age creates for social research.
* Use essential programming and computational techniques to analyse digital forms of social data.
* Understand the logics behind large language models and artificial intelligence.
* Practice sociological imaginations on the new social problems that we face in the age of big data and artificial intelligence.

courses.subject: SOCI
courses.title: Environment and Society
courses.description: This course provides a framework for understanding the relationship between human societies and the physical environment. It introduces students to research in environmental sociology and explores how sociological perspectives illuminate the ways human activity has contributed to the current ecological crisis. The course presents a broad spectrum of theoretical perspectives used by environmental sociologists to analyze and evaluate efforts to mitigate global and local environmental problems. It also promotes awareness and understanding of the Sustainable Development Goal framework, encouraging reflection on how individuals and societies can work together to address environmental challenges.
courses.learning_outcomes:
After completing this course, students should be able to:

1. Analyze and compare major theoretical approaches in environmental sociology
2. Apply conceptual tools to examine how social institutions could mitigate or exacerbate environmental problems
3. Evaluate claims related to environmental and natural resource issues from environmentalists, the business sector, and policymakers

courses.subject: SOCI
courses.title: Social Studies of Science
courses.description: In the past decades, the world witnessed a sea change in the influence that scientific knowledge has in shaping the course of our economy and culture. From the development of iPhone to the Covid-19 vaccine, ours is a society organized around and even controlled by expert knowledge systems. Meanwhile, science itself also underwent increasingly fiercer attack by outsiders, a typical example being the disagreement on whether the climate change is real. Sociology of scientific knowledge offers us a set of tools to understanding and analyzing these heatedly debated big questions in society today. By critically analyzing science, scientists, and the system of knowledge production that they created and maintained, this course surveys major topics and recent developments in the social studies of science. The arrangement of these topics follows a micro-to-macro sequence: First, the course discusses personal level STEM experiences, such as STEM educational pipeline and STEM job market; then, the course discusses institutional and disciplinary level analysis of how science works and how innovations come about; lastly, the course moves on to society, national, and international level of the impact of science, using examples from the platform economy, the international politics and the global pandemic. By the end of the course, students will develop basic knowledge towards the Technology Facilitation Mechanism for supporting the Sustainable Development Goals, through critically examining the multistakeholder collaboration between scientific community, nation states and other stakeholders in the STI policy cycle.
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. get familiar with major theoretical approaches in sociology of science and sociology of knowledge

2. understand what is a novel discovery in science, what defines a science discipline and what is interdisciplinarity

3. understand different forms of stratification in STEM pipeline, such as gender, race and global inequalities in becoming a star scientist / most innovative research team

4. critically reflect on the knowledge-based, skill-driven economy, with an emphasis on understanding the labor process in the gig economy and other data-intensive industrial sectors (i.e. software programming, AI)
5. critically reflect on the public mistrust in science during global crises, such as global warming and the Covid-19 pandemic
6. develop relevant knowledge towards the technology facilitation mechanism for supporting the Sustainable Development Goals

courses.subject: SOCI
courses.title: Introduction to Global Sociology
courses.description:
(UGEC3893 is double-coded with SOCI3241.)
This course introduces undergraduate students to the knowledge world of global and transnational sociology, and the sociology of law, globalization and global governance, in which it will offer a guided entry into the complex ideas and writings of the sociological, political and legal scholars. The concepts they developed to analyze law, society and global governance are fundamental to address global and international concerns, including ESG (environmental, social & governance) investment, SDG (sustainable development goals), global climate change (Paris Agreement), global health (WHO), international economic, trade and monetary institutions (e.g., World Bank, WTO, IMF), global gender, children, labor and human rights systems (UNICEF, ILO, OHCHR), international law (ICJ) and international peace and security (UN Security Council). Upon completing this course, student will have critical understanding of the process in which UN intergovernmental bodies, states and non-governmental stakeholders coordinate at the inter-organizational and inter-institutional levels to implement the UN 2030 Agenda, and respond to the challenges within.

courses.course_syllabus:
Topic 1: Introduction to Global and Transnational Sociology

Topic 2: Theory and Methods – Fieldwork, Ethnography and Participant Observation

* Question: How does the concept of exchange evolve from Mauss and Malinowski to Lévi-Strauss, Bourdieu, Fei Xiaotong and Dezalay? /中外学者如何抽象学术问题？

Topic 3: Theory and Methods – Historical/Economic/Institutional Sociology and Sociolegal Studies

* Question: How do academic scholars conduct global, transnational or comparative studies? /中外学者如何进行全球/跨国比较研究？

Topic 4: Global Law-Making and International Organizations (IOs) (SDG16, 17)

* Question: Why IOs are so relevant in global, transnational or comparative studies?

SDGs: the topic will enhance students’ knowledge in order to make sense of IOs and their custodianship of various sustainable development goals

Topic 5: Global Economy – Global Empire/World Bank

* Question: Who controls the World Bank and the IMF? What is the difference between the British and American Empires? And, why do the World Bank and IMF matter to an international financial hub like London, New York or Hong Kong?

Topic 6: International Money and Banking – IMF

* Question: who manages the global financial system? What is a financial crisis? And, how did it impact on international financial hubs like London, New York or Hong Kong?

Topic 7: Global Health and Education – WHO, MSF, and UNESCO (SDG3, 4)

* Question: what are the social factors of global health/education inequality?
* SDGs: the topic will examine the role of IOs that promote inclusive and equitable quality education and health and well-being.

Topic 8: International Trade and Investment – WTO and ICSID

* Question: What is the meaning of a free port? Why is it so important for Hong Kong to keep a free port trade policy? How did the US-China trade wars occur? What is the impact of the US-China trade wars on Hong Kong?

Topic 9: International Security and Peace – UNSC, IAEA, OPCW, and others (SDG16, 17)

* Question: what is the impact of UNSC on Hong Kong’s international banking system?
* SDGs: the topic will give the students a rare perspective towards how Hong Kong’s role as a financial centre can help achieve global security targets.

Topic 10: International Law and Justice – ICJ, ICC, ICTY, IOMed (SDG16, 17)

* Question: Why IOMed/ICC is interested in setting up an office in East Asia?
* SDGs: the topic will discuss how international partnership is core to achieving global security targets.

Topic 11: Gender, Children, Labor and Human Rights (SDG5, 8, 16)

* Question: what are the functions of global human rights bodies in the global community?
* SDGs: the topic will explore pathways towards enhancing gender equality, institutionalizing descent work settings and reducing crime and violence.

Topic 12: Global Climate Change and the Paris Agreement (SDG13)

* Question: what are the expected roles of Hong Kong and Mainland China in global action to climate change?
* SDGs: the topic will provide students a up-to-date view of the policy mechanism to achieve climate action targets in the region.

Topic 13: Environmental, Social, and Governance (ESG) and Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) (SDG17)

* Question: what are the expected roles of Hong Kong and Mainland China in ESG/SDGs?
* SDGs: as a summary, the topic will examine the institutionalization of SDGs in the case of a Chinese society

courses.subject: SOCI
courses.title: Crime and Social Control in Globalized Societies
courses.description: To Sutherland and Cressey, criminology is the body of knowledge regarding crime as social phenomena, which encompasses the process of making laws, breaking laws, and reacting to the breaking of laws. With a comparative perspective, this course focuses on how governments and law enforcement agencies in different societies intervene in the breaking of laws in a globalized world. The course begins by introducing various concepts of social control from a sociological standpoint. In addition to a review of the relevant criminological theories and empirical research findings, the course assesses the major impact of globalization on public security and explores the proliferation of transnational criminal activities in the process. Finally, the course will examine how social control agencies react and seek to address these problems in local and global context.
courses.learning_outcomes:
By the end of the course, the students are expected to:

1. have acquired a good knowledge on how criminological theories can help them to understand the relationship between crime and social control
2. understand the risks and challenges posed to public security in different societies by the process of globalization
3. recognize how some social control measures are conducted locally and in overseas to combat against crimes
4. be able to apply sociological perspectives and insights to review and assess the effectiveness of social control measures in different jurisdictions

courses.subject: SOCI
courses.title: Generalized Experimental Methods for Social Research
courses.description:
This course is a nontechnical introduction to the intellectual and methodological developments that undergird the exciting new era of social research using randomized experimental and quasi-experimental designs. Through in-depth case studies of exemplary empirical studies based on randomized experimental/quasi-experimental designs, the course will demonstrate how basic research could help understand human attitudes/perceptions/behaviors and provide evidence-based guidance for policymaking.

This course is intended for students in their third or fourth year of college. The course coverage will be helpful to students who intend to pursue more advanced academic training, government sector jobs that place a premium on program evaluation and quantitative literacy, or service industry jobs that have witnessed a surging demand for big data applications. A one-term course on basic statistics is essential for understanding the technical but elementary terms used in this course. Still, math and statistical skills are not required to excel in this course.

Specifically, the course is designed to achieve three learning objectives:
1. To master the generalized experimental methodology by studying the core principles of experimental designs and some exemplary social science experimental studies.
2. To ask real-world questions that can be answered with randomized experiments (of various forms) to understand human perceptions/behaviors, induce behavioral changes, and inform evidence-based policymaking.
3. To gain intuitive understanding of the counterfactual paradigm for causal thinking (i.e., using quasi-experimental designs) when truly randomized experiments are infeasible.

Evaluation of learning progress and performance will closely align with the course objectives. The course assessments would emphasize the conceptual and empirical understandings of experimental designs and their real-world applications through a group project presentation and a short research proposal written by individuals. The primary language of instruction, teaching materials, and students’ written submissions will be in English.

2. To ask real-world questions that can be answered with randomized experiments (of various forms) to understand human perceptions/behaviors, induce behavioral changes, and inform evidence-based policymaking.
3. To gain intuitive understanding of the counterfactual paradigm for causal thinking (i.e., using quasi-experimental designs) when truly randomized experiments are infeasible.
courses.course_syllabus:
* *Introduction**

1. Experimental thinking: What are experiments; how to use them to address real social issues and inform policy debates; core principles of experimental designs

* *Randomized experiments:**

2. Different types of randomized experiments: field experiments, lab experiments, and online experiments, etc. (provided with research exemplars) I
3. Different types of randomized experiments: field experiments, lab experiments, and online experiments, etc. (provided with research exemplars) II
4. Different types of randomized experiments: field experiments, lab experiments, and online experiments, etc. (provided with research exemplars) III
5. The synergies between experiments and traditional surveys
6. When experiments meet public policy interventions
7. When experiments meet behavioral science: conducting experiments in the digital age
8. Leveraging AI in experimental research

* *What can we do when classical randomized experiments are infeasible?**

9. Quasi-experiments: What it is and is not; the pitfalls of social experiments.
10. Weighting techniques as a statistical approach to emulate an experiment.
11. Matching techniques
12. Instrumental variables
13. Regression discontinuity designs

* *Asking Your Questions and Designing Your Experiments**

14. Group presentations
courses.feedback_evaluation:
Feedback for evaluation and improvement can be done between teacher and students via supervision.

The grade descriptors for this course are as follows:

Grade A: Outstanding performance on all learning outcomes.

Grade A-: Generally outstanding performance on all (or almost all) learning outcomes.

Grade B: Substantial performance on all learning outcomes, OR high performance on some learning outcomes which compensates for less satisfactory performance on others, resulting in overall substantial performance.

Grade C: Satisfactory performance on most learning outcomes, possibly with a few weaknesses.

Grade D: Barely satisfactory performance on a few learning outcomes.

Grade F: Unsatisfactory performance on a few learning outcomes, OR failure to meet specified assessment requirement.

courses.subject: SOCI
courses.title: Sociology of Housing
courses.description: Having access to adequate housing is a basic human right under Article 25 of the United Nations Universal Declaration of Human Rights (1948). Housing not only satisfies ontological needs but also plays a critical role in social stability and individual well-being. Decades later, housing problems in both local and overseas societies remain acute. However, we now have a better understanding of the social causes and consequences of these issues through years of empirical research and studies, hence with potential ways to achieve more sustainable housing. This course examines housing from a broad social science perspective while providing students with a solid foundation in the sociological understanding of housing. The course begins with a review of the literature, exploring the political economy of housing production and consumption. Key topics include: An examination of the various agents involved in the housing market, including tenants, landlords, urban planners, and developers. Reflections on the social construction of the meaning of housing and its implications for individuals and communities. A discussion on the financialization of the housing market and the unequal accessibility of mortgage credits, which shape life chances and create new forms of inequality in urban centers. The impact of living in substandard housing on health and psychological well-being. Issues of housing segregation along ethnic and racial lines, gentrification, displacement, and homelessness. The complex relationship between housing and welfare policy. Through this comprehensive examination, students will gain insights into the multifaceted nature of housing issues and the critical role of sociology in studying them.
courses.learning_outcomes:
L1 – To critically understand housing rights from social science perspectives

L2 – To analyse the housing markets and inequalities from comparative perspectives

L3 – To evaluate housing’s social impact on individuals and communities based on empirical studies

L4 – To examine sustainable housing policy and its complex relationship with welfare governance

L5 – To reflect on one’s value position and develop advocacy skills

courses.subject: SOCI
courses.title: Community Service-Based Learning for Sociology Students
courses.description:
Service-learning is a form of experiential learning in that students engage in cycle of service, knowledge application and reflection. This course provides Sociology students with an array of opportunities to engage in an immersive community service-learning experience. Working with community partners under the guidance of course instructor as well as mentors (in-service Sociology alumni teachers or community workers), students will complete specific projects and volunteer activities catered for specific learning needs of a student group in real-life setting, like teaching/tutoring/sharing knowledge, or designing a series of lessons particular topic on particular topic or problem the selected student group encountered.

Using the sociological imagination, students will identify the problem/ topic, gather information, design teaching/ learning objectives and outcomes, adopt appropriate delivering strategies, implement the plan with collaboration with their fellows and community partners. Upon completion of the practicum, students will put their sociological knowledge into dialogue with the experiential input from the service-learning setting, activities, and community partners through structured in-class discussions.
courses.learning_outcomes:
Students are able to:

1. consolidate and deepen the sociological knowledge they have acquired from the major curriculum in the course of teaching their secondary counterparts.
2. appreciate the value of serving the community and understand how sociological knowledge could contribute to the improvement of the community.
3. make use of effective collaborative learning process to advance information search and problem resolution.
4. cultivate empathic understanding of social needs of the disadvantaged groups in context and engage with outside partners to address social issues and develop social resolutions.
courses.course_syllabus:
Topics:

* Introduction: Course objectives and course structure
+ Sociological Imagination
+ Community-based Service learning
+ Applying Sociology in School Setting
* Workshop on selected skills: Cultural sensitivity, Information collection, Interview skills, Lesson design, planning, execution etc.
* Practicum under guidance of instructor and on-site mentors
* Reflection through structured in-class discussion

courses.subject: SOCI
courses.title: Religion in Modern Society
courses.description:
Many sociologists predicted that religion would gradually fade away in modern society and ‘modernity’ would eventually triumph over the ‘enchanted world’. On the contrary, the revival of traditional religions, blooming of new religious cults/sects, wide spread charismatic movement and fundamentalist movement of various sorts are taking place. Some contemporary sociologists therefore argued that we are in an age of ‘re-enchantment’ instead of ‘disenchanted’ modern society.

The course will be divided in two parts. In the first part, we will start from Max Weber’s theory to see how sociologists conceived the relation between religion and modernity as well as their conflicts. In the second part, we will go through contemporary issues concerning religious fundamentalism, religion and society in East Asia, China and Hong Kong.
courses.enrollment_requirement: Not for students who have taken SOCI4352 or CURE3112.
courses.learning_outcomes:
Untitled Document 1. A general understanding of the major debates in sociology of religion.
2. A grasp of major issues concerning religion and society in contemporary world, with special references to China.
3. Through assignments and reading materials, students are expected to enhance (a) the ability to formulate meaningful research question; (b) demonstrate the ability to conduct your social research project, and ( c) enhance the ability to appreciate quality sociological articles/readings.
4. Study tours will be conducted to provide students with basic understanding and appreciation of artifacts related to religions, including buildings and their locations, images (and where people put them), icons (colors, size, postures, etc.), liturgy, paintings, in the perspectives of sociology of religion.

courses.subject: SOCI
courses.title: Sociology Internship: Nurturing Competency for Meeting Future Challenges
courses.description: Internship is a valuable educational experience that nurtures students’ competency for responding to challenges in the 21st Century. Integrating academic curriculum with supervised internship, this course provides students with the opportunity to make sense of real-life work experiences with the sociological imagination. Course seminars aim at two objectives: to advance students’ career preparedness with exposure to work, and to transform their sociological knowledge into sustainability skills that are essential for promoting organisational change for a sustainable future. Aside from gaining on-the-job skills, students will learn to apply sociological analysis to study their internship organisations in both private or public sectors. Utilizing critical thinking – a core feature of sociological training, students will identify and reflect on specific values, goals, workflow and structure, and social and environmental impacts of an organisation through their first-hand experiences in various professions. To successfully enrol this course, students must secure their local internship arrangement through obtaining approval from the field supervisor and the course instructor. Students must complete a minimum of 100 hours of supervised internship work aside from the regular course assignments and attending course seminars.
courses.learning_outcomes:
The course’s learning outcomes are built around: raising career awareness, applying sociology knowledge, developing sustainability skills and the ability to promote positive change in the workplace. Upon completing the course, students should be able to:

* Demonstrate an in-depth knowledge of the internship organisation with the sociological imagination.
* Apply knowledge and methodological skills acquired in the sociology programme to real work environment.
* Outline the skills acquired from the job exposure and reflect on one’s competency.
* Appreciate one’s professional contributions to the internship organisation / related sector.
* Utilize sustainability skills to help address issues in the workplace or the community.
* Think critically about and explain how social factors shapes the workplace experience.
* Reflect on one’s value position and civic and workplace engagement.
* Identify ethical issues in a workplace and can make informed decisions on these issues.
courses.course_syllabus:
* *Course Structure**

The course consists of two parts. In the first part, students are required to attend seminars designed to advance their career competency in the following area: job planning, employment preparedness and work ethics, a readiness to apply sociological knowledge and methodological training for problem-solving in the workplace, and the development of sustainability skills such as conceptualizations of *Corporate Social Responsibility* (CSR), *Equity, Diversity, and Inclusion* (EDI), and the emerging *Social, Environment and Governance* (ESG) for professional workplace. The second part is the supervised internship. Students will complete a minimum of 100 hours of supervised work.

* *Course Component I: Seminars (at least five out of six total)**

Students are expected to participate in **at least five** (students must attend Seminar 1, 2, 5 and 6, and **optional for either Seminar 3 or Seminar 4**) regular in-class seminars.

* *Seminar 1 Getting started: sociology internship and experiential learning; knowing yourself: sociological imagination and preparing for a lifelong career**

* *Seminar 2 Knowing your workplace: organisational structure and culture**

* *Seminar 3 –Developing skills for sustainable future – Environmental Competence: conceptualizing *Corporate Social Responsibility* (CSR), *Environment, Social, and Governance* (ESG) and *Sustainable Development Goals* (SDGs)**

* *Seminar 4 – Developing skills for sustainable future – Cultural Competence: conceptualizing *Equity, Diversity, and Inclusion* (EDI) in workplace**

* *Seminar 5 – Knowing your workmate: social ties and social capital in the workplace; presentation skill for workplace**

* *Seminar 6: Presentation**

* *Course Component II: Supervised Internship**

* *Supervised Internship**

Students are required to complete 100 hours of supervised internship work experience at their approved internship site. Students will be required to have a weekly timesheet signed by their field supervisor and the timesheet will be submitted to the course instructor from time to time for tracking of work hours and keeping it up-to-date. Towards the end of the internship, the field supervisor will assess the intern’s work performance with a standardized performance evaluation sheet in consultation with the course instructor. Student must pass the workplace performance evaluation, which contributes to a maximum 20% of the overall course marks, in order to complete the course.

* *Course Internship Policy**

Terms of reference:

According to the Minimum Wage Ordinance (Cap. 608), Statutory Minimum Wages does not apply to the following student employees:

* **Student Interns**; and
* **Work Experience Students** during a period of exempt student employment.

For detail, please referring to the following website:

Labour Department. Statutory Minimum Wage: Notes for Student Employees and Employers (English version)

<https://www.labour.gov.hk/eng/news/pdf/Notes_for_Student_Employees_and_Employers.pdf>

Labour Department. Statutory Minimum Wage: Notes for Student Employees and Employers (Chinese version)

<https://www.labour.gov.hk/tc/public/pdf/smw/SMW_Notes_for_Students_Employees_and_Employers_tc.pdf>

* *Course Assignments**

One student portfolio (5%) and one e-portfolio (5%)

Two reflective journals (15% each; total 30%)

Presentation (10%)

Final written report (30%)

Field supervisor assessment (20%)

courses.subject: SOCI
courses.title: Sociology and Modern Life
courses.description: This course explores key concepts, theories, and perspectives essential for sociological analysis. Students will develop a distinctive sociological imagination that differentiates sociological thinking from other disciplinary approaches. The course examines the relationship between individuals and social institutions throughout historical contexts, from early modernity to late modernity. Topics include sociological perspectives, self and social interaction, deviance, social stratification, work, urban life, global inequality, and environmental issues. By the end of the course, students will be able to identify major sociological perspectives, think sociologically, apply concepts to everyday social phenomena, and critically evaluate the strengths and limitations of sociological theories.
courses.learning_outcomes:
After completing this course, students should be able to:

1. Identify selected sociological perspectives and sociological key concepts.
2. Demonstrate the ability to think in a sociological way.
3. Apply different sociological perspectives/key concepts to social issues in everyday life
4. Evaluate the applicability of sociological theories by critically analyzing their strengths and limitations in explaining contemporary social phenomena.

1. “The Promise”: Navigating between Personal Troubles and Public Issues
2. The Birth of the Sociological Way of Thinking
3. Modernity and the Development of Sociology
4. Doing Sociology
5. Self and Social Interaction
6. Normality and Deviance
7. Stratification and Differentiation
8. Work and Employment
9. Cities and Urban Life
10. Development and Global Inequality
11. Environmental Consequences of Modernity
12. "The Promise" Revisited

courses.subject: SOCI
courses.title: Methods of Social Inquiry
courses.description:
This course provides a survey of the most commonly used methods, both qualitative and quantitative, in social research. It reviews the basic principles, procedures, objectives, and ethics of conducting social search. It introduces the common data collection and analysis methods, including survey, experiment, interview, observation, ethnography, big data, statistical analysis, causal inference, case study, content analysis, and process tracing. These methods will also be applied in group research projects. By taking this course, students will:

• understand what social research is and know the ethics of social research;• be familiar with the common social research methods;• know how to appreciate and evaluate existing social research; and
• practice social research and learn to present and write research findings.
courses.course_syllabus:
1. Introduction
2. Research Basics and Research Design
3. Survey
4. Experiment
5. Observation & Ethnography
6. Interview; Archives; Big Data
7. Statistical Analysis & Causal Inference (I)
8. Statistical Analysis & Causal Inference (II)
9. Case Study & Content Analysis
10. Process Tracing; Mixed Methods
11. Group Project Presentation & Discussion (I)
12. Group Project Presentation & Discussion (II)
13. Course Summary
courses.feedback_evaluation: By a common Department course evaluation.

courses.description: This course discusses the basic principles of statistical reasoning and their applications in social research. Basic techniques for descriptive and inferential analysis will be discussed to enhance students’ ability in analysing associations between social variables in the context of theory and hypothesis testing. Students will be given opportunities to use statistical software for analysing survey data. No prior computing experience is required.
courses.learning_outcomes:
The learning outcomes include:
(1) The role of quantitative evidence in scientific reasoning,
(2) Ability to articulate (verbally and in writing) the fundamental principles of statistical reasoning in relation to the role of quantitative evidence,
(3) Basic concepts and tools of statistics with special reference to the concept of spurious relationship and confounding bias, and
(4) Statistical literacy (ability to interpret the statistical results commonly reported in the mass media and applied social research, to recognize and avoid some common misuse of statistics).

2. Descriptive statistics
3. Inferential statistics
4. Comparing group means
5. Simple regression analysis
6. Multiple regression analysis
7. Modelling techniques

courses.subject: SOCI
courses.title: Contemporary Chinese Society
courses.description:
The course will discuss the impetus of institutional change in contemporary China (1949 -) and its impacts on social and political lives of Chinese people. Key social science concepts such as
ideology, socialism, marketization, patron-clientelism, social control, civil society, industrialization, urbanization, class formation, social inequality, gender, family and special terms such as guanxi,
danwei, hukou, floating population, three-represents, harmonious society, China's dream. etc. will be introduced.
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. Students should be able to understand the social science perspective used in this course to comprehend the main aspects of social change in contemporary China. They should be familiar with the key concepts and theories adopted from sociology and other social science disciplines in the analysis.

2. Students are expected to grasp the basic knowledge about China's major institutions such as socialist market economy, party-state politics, stratification and family etc.

3. Besides general knowledge, students are encouraged to focus on a special topic through their writing assignment such as the dilemmas between economic development and social equality, democracy and developmental state, traditional culture and modernization etc.
courses.course_syllabus:
Week 1: Introduction: Basic Information of the Chinese Society

\* Provide basic information about the socio-economic characteristics of the contemporary China

Week 2 & 3: China before Reform Era

\* Provide information about the political systems, economic institutions, and social control mechanisms of China during Mao’s era

Week 4 & 5: Economic Reform

\* Provide information about the processes of economic reform since 1978, as well as its impact on the economic system afterwards

Week 6 & 7 & 8: Political Reform and the Preservation of the Party-state

\* Provide information about the impact of the economic reform on the political system in China afterwards. Also, we will discuss the puzzle regarding the preservation of the Party-state despite the massive political reform.

Week 9: Rural-urban Development and Migration

\* Provide information about the differential impact of the economic reform on the urban and rural development, as well as the resultant great migration since then.

Week 10: Social Stratification and Inequality

\* Provide information about the impact of the economic reform on the transition of the social stratification system and the enlarging social inequality.

Week 11 & 12: Social Conflicts, Civil Society, and Social Control

\* Provide information about the impact of the economic reform on the growing social conflicts and the newly emerged social control mechanisms.

Week 13: Population and Demographic Transition

\* Provide information about the impact of the economic reform on the population and demographic patterns.

courses.subject: SOCI
courses.title: Globalization: Challenges and Responses

meetings.location: Chung Chi College Lib CK TSE
Chung Chi College Lib CK TSE

courses.description: Globalization has emerged as one of the most talked about concepts in the contemporary world. This course takes globalization as the master trend leading to profound changes in social life everywhere and will examine its multi-faceted manifestations in economic, political and cultural dimensions. Guided by the sociology of development, this course will seek to develop a broad interdisciplinary approach to the academic debates over globalization. Particular attention will also be paid to the challenges that globalization poses on human development and the responses that the global community has fashioned to tackle these challenges.
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. To understand the complex and multi-dimensional nature of globalization from sociological and multi-disciplinary approaches.
2. To comprehend the major academic debates arising from the process of globalization and locate current issues in the context of these debates.
3. To reflect on the impact of globalization on human development as well as the responses of the global community.
4. To master the skills in conducting analytical research on globalization issues.
5. To relate academic and community debates on globalization to our everyday life and formulate individual and collective strategies in responding to globalization and its impacts.
courses.course_syllabus:
1. What is Globalization?

a.Nature of globalization: when, what and how.

b.Globalization and the Sociology of Development

c.What are the Debates About? Opposing views on globalization.

2. Political, Social and Economic Dimensions of Globalization

3. Individual and Collective Responses to Globalization

a. A basic understanding of the United Nations’ Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs)

courses.description: Seemingly, citizens of modern society live with increasing affluence and liberty. Yet lurking behind prosperity and development is the widening of regional and social inequalities, the persistence of social exclusion and discrimination, and the emergence of new forms of social problems. Using insights from a variety of sociological theories, this course engages students in sociological debates about the meanings, patterns, causes, and consequences of these issues, and their impact on our lives.

courses.subject: SOCI
courses.title: Issues in Education and Society
courses.description: This course examines the relationship between education institution and the society. It offers a sociological perspective on social processes and outcomes of schooling. Topics such as major sociological theories on education, their application in analyzing the structure and processes of schooling as an organization and an institution, effects of education on societal level (the function of allocation and selection of education, credential system etc.) as well as the individual level (such as socialization and social stratification) are covered in the course. Empirical studies in Hong Kong, China as well as other societies will be adopted as illustration of theoretical analysis and application. Upon the completion of the course, students are expected to have basic understanding of sociology of education, and the capability to reflect on the meaning and significance of education and schooling experiences in a sociological way.
courses.course_syllabus:
1. What is Sociology of Education
2. Major Sociological Theories on Education
3. Social Stratification and Education: Effects of Education on Individual Level
4. Education as Social Certification: Effects of Education on Societal Level
5. Education in Knowledge Society
6. Education in Modern Society
7. Education in Network Society
8. Education in World Society
9. Education reform and social changes

courses.subject: SOCI
courses.title: Doing Class in Everyday Life
courses.description: Although some theorists claim that ‘class is dead’ and thus fails to explain contemporary society, our daily life is still influenced by class factors, not only in terms of resource allocation but also in the ways we conduct our social life. In this course, we will explore how class shapes our lifestyles, housing, association patterns, life chances, education strategies, parent-child interaction, subjective identification and even political orientation. We do not simply take class as objective structural positions; we also perceive class as crucial social boundaries and distinctions that have to be practiced, performed and reproduced in various social fields. Such a perspective will help us to discover and reflect how our social life is ‘classed’.
courses.course_syllabus:
1. Introduction
2. The Classical Approaches: Marx vs. Weber
3. The Contemporary Approaches: John Goldthorpe, E. O. Wright and Pierre Bourdieu
4. Social Mobility
5. Social Networks and Social Capital
6. Poverty
7. Class Identification and Class Consciousness
8. Conclusion

courses.subject: SOCI
courses.title: Thesis Project
courses.description: This thesis project course provides students a hands-on opportunity to complete an individual research on a topic of interest under the instructor’s supervision. Students will be able to gain the firsthand experience in developing a research project and to consolidate their knowledge and skills in research methods. Through classroom trainings and individual consultations, the instructor will provide appropriate guidance to students during each stage of the research process and thesis writing.
courses.learning_outcomes:
Upon completing this course, students will have acquired in-depth knowledge on the topic relating to the current theoretical controversies, research issues, and methodological approaches in sociology. The specific course objectives include:

(1) To broaden the knowledge in a chosen field of study
(2) To analyze and integrate research literature in the field
(3) To apply the sociological perspective to the research project
(4) To strengthen research methods skills
(5) To complete a research report in an appropriate format
courses.course_syllabus:
There will be weekly trainings for various stages of research and thesis writing, including but not limited to the following:
1 Formulating research questions
2 Literature review
3 Conceptualization
4 Research design and timeline
5 Data and method
6 Data analysis
7 Thesis writing: structuring, drafting and revising

In addition, the instructor will be available for individual consultations.

courses.subject: SOCI
courses.title: Advanced Theory
courses.description:
This course is primarily intended to give graduate students a detailed introduction to some foundational theoretical questions and positions in sociology. It is centered on the writings of Max Weber, Emile Durkheim, and Karl Marx, supplemented by other (and more recent) theoretical work. The readings and seminar discussions will cover three mains sets of issues: 1) the relationship between the individual and society; 2) the special challenges and problems confronting a science of society; and 3) theoretical accounts of key modern social structures. Secondarily, students will through a series of exercises leading up to a final paper gain practice in the essential skill of “reading and writing with theories.”

The course grade will be based on a term paper, which will be assigned a letter grade according to following rubric:
The “significant progress” mentioned in the rubric will be judged in reference to the writing exercises completed during the second half of the course but which will not be graded separately.
courses.learning_outcomes:
1. to critically engage with different classical positions on how to conceptualize the connection between individuals and social relations or institutions
2. to confront the basic problems of what it means to have a science of society, and how this relates back to social life
3. to introduce students to the classic, and some more contemporary, interpretations of large-scale social transformations that characterize “modern” society
4. through a series of exercises leading up to a term paper, to train students in the essential skill of “reading and writing with theories”
courses.course_syllabus:
Part I: The Individual and Society

1. Cooperation and Exchange
2. Social Solidarity and the Division of Labor
3. Social Change, Social Functions, and Their Dysfunction
4. The Individual against Society
5. Gendered Individuals

Part II: Theoretical Legacies of Marx and Weber

1. The State as Organized Coercion
2. The Powers of the State
3. Classical Theories of Capitalism
4. Neo-Marxism: Class and Politics
5. Debating Theories of Class

Part III: The Problem of a Science of Society

1. Are Social Facts “Things”?
2. Sociology, Interests, and Values
